dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 7 responding for an unconscious adult on a bench. Call time is at 16:40.
Hi, I'm Jordan, an EMS responder with the county EMS. Can you explain what happened here?
He's been unconscious since I called EMS earlier today. I found him slumped over in his car.
Sir, can you wake up for me? What's your name?
No eye opening with verbal stimuli at this point.
He is not responding to verbal stimuli.
No motor response after gentle shoulder tap.
Radial pulse is 91, strong and regular.
Pulse ox is 99% on ambient air.
Irregular respirations at 8 per minute with snoring sounds.
Blood pressure is 124 over 67.
Airway is patent but with some snoring.
Breathing is irregular with some effort but normal depth.
Warm, dry skin, normal color and cap refill under 2 seconds.

dialogue.micro_intent: S.A.M.P.L.E. events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: EMS was here earlier, but the patient refused help. He was in his car when it happened.
Next, I'll assess his GCS. He's got an initial GCS of 11, with eye = 2, verbal = 4, motor = 5.
EKG shows sinus rhythm.
Glucose level is 87 mg/dl.
Pupils are pinpoint and equal on both sides, reactive after initial awakening.
Chest rises equally and breath sounds are clear and equal bilaterally.
Both arms and legs have normal movement and coordination after regaining consciousness.
We're placing you on a cardiac monitor, starting capnography, and placing you in 4-lead monitoring.
We will start an IV on your left antecubital space with a 20-gauge catheter.
Administering 4 LPM of oxygen and a 1.0 mg dose of Narcan intravenously. You may feel more alert shortly.
EKG now shows sinus tachycardia at 112 bpm.
Heart rate has risen to 112 bpm.
Breathing is now regular and steady at 19 per minute.
Blood pressure is now 159 over 85.
Oxygen saturation is up to 100% on room air.
Glucose is now undetectable at 0 mg/dl.
No deformities, contusions, abrasions, punctures, tenderness, lacerations, or swelling noted during full-body assessment.
He is now alert and can follow commands like opening his eyes.
Eyes open to command now.
Speaking clearly and responding to questions.
He is moving both arms and legs to command with normal coordination.
He is now fully awake with a/ox4/4 - alert and oriented to all four.
He's now alert and oriented to person, place, and time, but appears restless and slightly agitated.
He's improved to GCS 15 - fully oriented in all categories.
Re-checking for injuries - still no signs of deformities, cuts, or swelling.
Capnography shows regular respiratory waveform and normal CO2.

dialogue.micro_intent: S.A.M.P.L.E. events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: He was found slumped over in his car earlier, seems the same event. He refused care then but now shows a significant change in mental status.
Previously, he was extremely stuporous with a GCS of 11, now he is alert and oriented.
Based on GCS change and opioid overdose signs, we are following protocol 7-1 for overdose/poisoning.
Repetition of vitals for reassurance of consistent response to Narcan.
We'll now assist you into a semi-Fowler's position for transport.
Using a stretcher with shoulder and lap belts to secure you safely.
You're declining care, but I must advise transport for observation. Your Refusal of Treatment is noted but we'll provide full documentation.
If you change your mind, we can reassess quickly and assist in a safe transfer to the hospital.
I will continue monitoring for any changes in your alertness or behavior during transport.
You remain normocephalic with no signs of facial distress or deformity.

dialogue.micro_intent: S.A.M.P.L.E. events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: Repeating prior events-found unconscious in his car and again unresponsive on the bench with worsening symptoms.
Sinus tach continues at 112 for now. Any sudden changes will prompt immediate review.
Airway remains patent and your breathing is noticeably improved since Narcan.
Re-checking lung fields, still clear and equal bilaterally, no added sounds.
You remain at GCS 15. Maintaining good contact and awareness.
You're still a/ox4/4 - can you tell me what city you're in? What's today's date? How long have you been sitting here?
Everything secured for transport in baseline semi-Fowler's.
Under protocol 7-1, transported patients are evaluated for longer monitoring, though you've already regained full responsiveness.
You've declined transport per patient signature. Documentation noted and time-stamped with your refusal decision.
We'll keep you monitored during transport. Your refusal remains on file for review.
You can still change your mind at any point before reaching the destination.
No signs of trauma in arms or legs, just more normal temperature regulation and dry skin.
You seem more agitated towards the end. We'll monitor closely.
Do you have any allergies that I should note on file before completing documentation?
I don't know his allergies, he wouldn't tell me anything earlier.
Have you taken any medications recently that could affect this condition?
I don't know, and he denied any type of drug or alcohol use too.
No signs of swallowing issues or obvious neck tenderness or lymph nodes present.
Our destination is predetermined, but he is declining treatment at the nearest hospital.
Is there any prior serious injury or illness we should be aware of for documentation?
No, he's free of serious past illness or conditions, if he said anything before.
EKG remains stable in sinus tach, no signs of increasing rhythm change for now.
No drooling or secretions, mouth appears normal, teeth intact, no bite marks or debris.
Rechecking the straps showed no shift or instability. Movement is clear.
Again, both upper and lower extremities show normal motor and sensory function.
No issues with movement during transfer-this will be recorded for final report.
You're warming up again-you feel more restless. Let's keep eyeing the pulse and the SpO2 to note if we see more signs.
No signs of periorbital edema, equal pressure behind both eyes.
When did you last have something to eat or drink?
I don't know. He wouldn't tell me anything when they first came, and he refused again.
Glucose still reads zero. We note this in our final report.
You are still under observation and deemed safe to transport under the protocol advised.
Protocol 7-1 is clear. We've completed all initial treatments and note refusal by patient after stabilizing.
Skin appears normal in texture, color, and moisture-no signs of rash, jaundice, or cyanosis at this time.
Any symptoms returning or signs of worsened breathing, we'll be right there with second dose Narcan.
I am still catching a little bit of restlessness. I'll re-check capnography and etCO2 for accuracy.
We've moved him on ramp for loading, still secured in semi-Fowler's, no loosening in harness.
Blood pressure remains stable at 159/85 mmHg at record time.

dialogue.role: nose, mouth and throat exam; EMT
dialogue.utterance: No signs of discharge or blockage in nose. Ears untouched. Throat clear upon visual.
If necessary, documentation can be forwarded to physician at hospital regardless of refusal.
No crackles, wheezes, or rhonchi-chest wall exam stable and non-tender.
Transportation time remains around 15 minutes from here to nearest receiving hospital.
Patient employing verbal affirmation correctly, no issues with secondary orientation tests.
No signs of extrication related injuries or movements that may suggest hidden trauma.
Capnography remains intact for baseline CO2 and waveform. Presumably full, no change.
No rigidity, distention, or guarding in abdomen-everything feels soft and non-tender.
Our communication to the hospital emergencies team is complete and pending any change.
Any conditions previously that we should know of in our handling documentation?
None, that I'm aware of. He didn't tell me anything about any condition or medication use.
Time-stamped and finalized the documentation under protocol 7-1 for overdose/poisoning.
